stories.title: Five independents join the deck
stories.summary: Studio Underd0g, Baltic, Trafford, William Wood, and Brew arrive on WatchSwipe — twenty watches spanning watermelon chronographs to firefighter heritage.
stories.body:
WatchSwipe's catalog just grew by five of the most-loved independent brands in modern watchmaking, each with its four most popular models.

Studio Underd0g (London, 2020) built a cult following in record time with pun-named dégradé dials — the Watermel0n chronograph is already an icon — every piece hand-assembled in Britain by a single technician.

Baltic (Paris, 2016) is the vintage-inspired standard-bearer of French microbrands: the Aquascaphe diver, the micro-rotor MR01 dress watch, the rugged little Hermétique, and the panda-dial Scalegraph, all assembled in Besançon.

Trafford (Austin, 2020) tells American stories in color — Hill Country wildflower dials on the Crossroads S, guitar-legend colorways on the Touring GMT, and a Kickstarter-born racing chronograph honoring WWII spy-drivers.

William Wood (Newcastle, 2017) honors the founder's firefighter grandfather: upcycled fire-hose straps, crowns cast from melted fire-helmet brass, and a range that runs from the Valiant diver to the Triumph chronograph.

Brew (New York, 2015) designs watches around the espresso ritual — chronograph scales sized to a 25–35 second pull, cushion cases inspired by vintage espresso machines. Coffee culture, on the wrist.

Every model and colorway is live in the deck now — swipe through and find your favorites.

stories.title: Astor + Banks brings Chicago watchmaking to WatchSwipe
stories.summary: The veteran-founded independent joins the deck — officer's watches, a dual-crown tool diver, and a field watch, all assembled in Chicago.
stories.body:
Astor + Banks has been assembling watches in Chicago since 2012, when Army veteran Andrew Perez named the brand after the Gold Coast street corner where he lived. The catalog that lands on WatchSwipe today shows how far an American independent can push at these prices.

The Fortitude line is the heart of it: officer's-style everyday watches at 38.5mm, with the Lite offering a slim no-date take at $650 and the Pro adding HV1200-hardened steel and 20,000A/m anti-magnetic protection. Both run Miyota automatics adjusted in-house.

At the tool end sits the Sea Ranger M2 — a dual-crown design whose internal 12-hour ring gives you a second time zone, rated to 1000ft and powered by a Swiss LJP G100 with a 68-hour reserve. The Terra Scout rounds out the range: a sandblasted field watch under box sapphire, with a full-lume dial option that glows edge to edge.

Swipe through all four models now, including every production colorway.

stories.title: Why the PRX Powermatic 80 is the gateway mechanical
stories.summary: Integrated bracelet, waffle dial, 80-hour reserve — how Tissot built the most recommended first automatic of the decade.
stories.body:
Ask ten collectors what a first automatic should be and the Tissot PRX Powermatic 80 shows up on most lists. The formula is simple: 1970s integrated-bracelet styling that reads far above its price, and the Powermatic 80 movement, whose 80-hour power reserve means the watch is still running on Monday morning after a weekend off the wrist.

The waffle-pattern dial nods to the 1978 original, while the slim 40mm case wears flat and comfortable under a cuff. It is not a small watch, but it is an easy one.

For swipe-happy WatchSwipe users: if your taste profile leans Sports, this is the one to try on first. The hardest part is picking a dial — ice blue has become the signature, but the green rewards a second look.

stories.title: The Moonwatch at 57: why the Speedmaster never changes
stories.summary: Omega's hand-wound chronograph is horology's least-changed icon. That's a feature, not a bug.
stories.body:
The Speedmaster Professional that flew on Apollo 11 is, in every way that matters, the watch Omega sells today. Hesalite crystal, hand-wound chronograph, tachymeter bezel — the Moonwatch resists updates the way other icons resist discontinuation.

Collectors argue about hesalite versus sapphire, but the acrylic crystal is the purist's pick: it is what NASA flight-qualified, and a tube of PolyWatch keeps it clear.

If the 42mm case and manual winding sound like commitment, that is rather the point. Few watches connect you to a specific human achievement every time you wind the crown.

stories.title: How the CasiOak became the default first G-Shock
stories.summary: The GA-2100 stripped the G-Shock to its essentials — and accidentally created a hype watch.
stories.body:
When Casio released the GA-2100 in 2019, the octagonal bezel earned it an instant nickname — the "CasiOak" — and an audience far beyond G-Shock loyalists. The carbon core guard let Casio shrink the case to its slimmest profile ever, so the watch finally slid under a shirt cuff.

At around $99 it became the answer to "what watch should I actually beat up?" The all-black colorway disappears on the wrist; the sand and red-accent versions have become the enthusiast picks.

Modders took it further with metal bezels and straps, but the stock resin version remains the purest expression: a tough, accurate, genuinely stylish watch for a dinner-for-two price.

stories.title: Prospex SPB143: the 62MAS reissue that got it right
stories.summary: Seiko's modern take on its 1965 first diver balances vintage proportions with a modern movement.
stories.body:
Seiko has reissued its 1965 62MAS diver many times, but the SPB143 is the version enthusiasts kept. At 40.5mm with a slim bezel and drilled lugs, it wears like the vintage original while the 6R35 movement brings a 70-hour reserve.

The sunburst grey dial shifts from charcoal to silver in changing light — subtle in a category that often shouts. 200m of water resistance and a sapphire crystal make it a genuine tool watch, not a costume.

It sits in a crowded Seiko dive lineup, but if you want one Seiko diver that does everything, this is the consensus pick.

stories.title: The case for hand-wound: living with the Khaki Field Mechanical
stories.summary: No date, no rotor, 38mm — Hamilton's field watch argues that less watch is more watch.
stories.body:
The Khaki Field Mechanical asks you to do something almost no modern product does: participate. Wind it in the morning — about thirty seconds of ritual — and it repays you with a full day of timekeeping and a case so light you forget it.

At 38mm with fixed bars and a matte dial, it is the closest thing to the original military spec watches Hamilton supplied. The 80-hour hand-wound movement is modern; everything else is 1965.

It has become the enthusiast consensus for "watch you keep forever under $600" — and the sage-strap version is quietly the best of them.

stories.title: Tank Must: how Cartier made its icon attainable again
stories.summary: The Must line revives Cartier's 1970s idea — the Tank as an accessible design object.
stories.body:
In the 1970s, Cartier's "Les Must de Cartier" line put the Tank silhouette within reach of a much wider audience. The modern Tank Must repeats the trick: quartz movements, steel cases, and those unmistakable Roman numerals, at a fraction of the price of the Louis Cartier.

The colored-dial versions — deep green, burgundy — sell out repeatedly, but the silver dial is the forever pick. It is not a sports watch, and 30m of water resistance means it should never see a pool.

Worn slim under a jacket, nothing else at the price signals design literacy quite like it.

stories.title: Case size, decoded: finding your millimeter
stories.summary: From 33mm Tanks to 45mm G-Shocks — how to read case size, lug-to-lug, and what actually fits.
stories.body:
Case diameter is the most quoted and least useful number in watches. What determines fit is lug-to-lug — the top-to-bottom span — and case thickness.

A rough guide: a 6.5-inch wrist comfortably takes a lug-to-lug up to about 48mm. A 40mm diver with short lugs can wear smaller than a 38mm field watch with long ones. Shaped watches like a rectangular Tank read even smaller than their numbers.

Your WatchSwipe taste profile tracks the average case size of watches you like — check Profile to see where your eye actually lands, then try that size on before buying up or down.

stories.title: Why mechanical watches keep growing in a smartwatch world
stories.summary: A decade after the smartwatch was supposed to kill it, the mechanical watch is thriving. The reasons say a lot about what people want on their wrists.
stories.body:
Every year since the Apple Watch launched, mechanical watch exports have set new records. The explanation is not nostalgia — it is differentiation. A smartwatch is a screen; a mechanical watch is a signature.

Younger buyers arrive through integrated-bracelet sports watches and reissued divers, then move up the ladder. Entry Swiss and Japanese brands — Tissot, Seiko, Hamilton — have never had stronger lineups, which is precisely why they anchor the WatchSwipe catalog.

The smartwatch did kill something: the boring quartz watch. What survived is everything with a point of view.

watches.description: A modern reissue of the 1960s 62MAS dive watch. Slim case, high-legibility dial, and a 70-hour automatic movement.
Integrated-bracelet sports watch with a waffle-pattern dial and an 80-hour Powermatic movement.
A hand-wound military field watch with a clean 24-hour dial and matte finish. Tool-watch heritage in a wearable size.
The Moonwatch. A hand-wound chronograph with a tachymeter bezel and a history that reaches the lunar surface.
The "CasiOak" — an octagonal-bezel G-Shock with a slim carbon-core build, shock resistance, and 200m water resistance.
An icon of dress watchmaking. The rectangular Tank silhouette with Roman numerals and a slim leather strap.

The civilian take on Astor + Banks' officer's-style Fortitude: slimmer case, clean no-date dial, and a Miyota 9039 automatic adjusted in-house. Applied markers and hands glow with BGW9 Super-LumiNova, and the tapered 20mm bracelet hides polished chamfers on the underside of the links. Anti-magnetic to the ISO 764 standard and water resistant to 660ft — an everyday watch that punches far above $650.

The flagship officer's watch, updated: steel hardened to HV1200 for scratch resistance, anti-magnetic to 20,000A/m — over four times the ISO requirement — and a Miyota 9015 automatic adjusted in-house. Heat-blued hands on select dials, BGW9 lume throughout, and the same perfectly tapered bracelet as the Lite. Formal to casual, 38.5mm, any day of the week.

watches.caseMaterial: Brushed 316L steel, HV1200 coating, soft-iron anti-magnetic plates
watches.description: A dual-crown, go-anywhere tool watch two years in the making. The top crown drives an internal 12-hour rotating ring — a second time zone without a GMT movement — while the Swiss LJP G100 automatic runs a 68-hour reserve, adjusted in-house to ±8 seconds a day. Rated to 1000ft with hardened HV1200 steel and a soft-iron anti-magnetic cage. Bracelet versions include a color-matched FKM rubber strap ($1,149 on strap only).

A modern interpretation of the classic military field watch: tough, legible, reliable. The 3-layer matte dial sits under box sapphire, the sandblasted 38.5mm case wears 46mm lug-to-lug, and the Swiss LJP G100 automatic is adjusted in-house to ±8 seconds a day with a 68-hour reserve. Ships with both canvas and FKM rubber straps and a Horween leather watch wallet. Desert Sand limited edition $1,195; the full-lume dial option glows edge to edge.

The 01Series is Studio Underd0g's third-generation flagship chronograph and the watch that put the brand on the map: a 38.5mm x 12.9mm 316L steel case driving a hand-wound Chinese Seagull ST-1901B bicompax movement (21,600vph, 50-hour power reserve with the chronograph stopped). Its lead dial, Watermel0n, uses a two-tone red-over-green dégradé finish that has been the brand's signature design since its 2020 launch. The case carries a sapphire crystal, 5 ATM (50m) water resistance, and a quick-release leather strap fitted by The Strap Tailor.

The 02Series is Studio Underd0g's homage to WWII-era "Dirty Dozen" military field watches, built around a 37.5mm x 11.5mm 316L steel case and a hand-wound Swiss Sellita SW200-2 M movement with a 63-hour power reserve and hacking seconds. Its lead colorway, Nightshade, pairs a sunburst deep-purple dial with aged orange numerals and a distressed leather strap to mimic decades of wear. The multi-layer dial uses a 1mm tinted sapphire top-disc over a sunray brass base with X1 Super-LumiNova lume, and the case is rated to 10 ATM (100m).

The 03Series is Studio Underd0g's premium monopusher chronograph, cased at 38.5mm x 13.6mm in 316L steel and powered by a hand-wound Sellita SW510 M (Spécial/Élaboré grade, ±5 s/d accuracy, 63-hour reserve) operated by a single cam-actuated pusher instead of the usual two. Its lead colorway, Salm0n, sets a salmon-pink tachymeter rim against a radial-brushed grey dégradé dial. At £1,750 it is the brand's highest-priced core-range model, rated to 5 ATM (50m) water resistance with a sapphire crystal.

The Cocktail Collection is a run of limited-edition collaborations between Studio Underd0g and historic British watchmaker Fears, sharing a 38mm x 12mm cushion-cased 316L steel case (10 ATM/100m) and a hand-wound Sellita SW200-2 M movement. Each release is named after a cocktail and sold in small batches at watch fairs: the lead colorway, Gimlet, was limited to 200 pieces at British Watchmakers' Day 2025 and reportedly caused a stampede on release, followed by the 300-piece Manhattan at WindUp Watch Fair NYC and The Mimosa. All three carry FEARS-branded dials with a sapphire crystal and typically sell out within their launch window.

Aquascaphe Classic is Baltic's signature dive watch and, per the brand's own "Most popular" homepage ranking, its best-selling model. The 39mm stainless steel case runs a Miyota 9039 automatic movement with a 42-hour power reserve, backed by 200m of water resistance and a double-dome sapphire crystal. Its sandwich dial construction — a Baltic hallmark — creates deep, three-dimensional markers at 3, 6 and 9 filled with Super-LumiNova C1, finished off with a 120-click unidirectional bezel and a screwed crown.

MR01 is Baltic's micro-rotor dress watch, the #2 model in the brand's own "Most popular" homepage ranking. It is built around the Hangzhou CAL5000a automatic micro-rotor caliber — a thin, self-winding movement usually reserved for far pricier dress watches — inside a 36mm stainless steel case topped with a high-domed hesalite crystal for a vintage look. The 42-hour power reserve movement is rated to just 30m water resistance, reflecting its dress-watch brief, and ships on an Italian calf leather strap. The line also includes a guilloché-dial "Roulette" variant alongside the plainer railroad-track "Classic" dial shown here.

Hermétique is Baltic's field/adventure watch, appearing more times than any other line in the brand's own "Most popular" homepage ranking. The compact 37mm stainless steel case runs a Miyota 9039 automatic movement with a 42-hour power reserve, under a double-dome sapphire crystal, with an integrated crown keeping the profile clean. It carries an unusually rugged 150m water resistance rating for a field watch. The same case and movement are sold in two dial-color families — the muted, canvas-inspired "Tourer" palette and the brighter, beach-toned "Summer" colorways.

Scalegraph Classic (still widely known by its earlier name, Tricompax) is Baltic's flagship three-register "panda dial" chronograph, the modern descendant of the twin-register Bicompax that helped launch the brand's 2017 debut. Its "Panda" colorway is the only chronograph to make Baltic's own "Most popular" homepage ranking. The 39.5mm stainless steel case is powered by a hand-wound Sellita SW510-M movement with a 63-hour power reserve, under a double-dome sapphire crystal and an aluminum tachymeter bezel, rated to 50m water resistance.

The Crossroads S is the newest generation of Trafford's flagship Crossroads line: a square case marketed as 'wears like 40mm' but actually 35mm x 36mm and just 9mm thick, with a 46mm lug-to-lug. It is powered by a hand-wound Sellita SW-210 built in La Chaux-de-Fonds and assembled in Porrentruy, the first Trafford watch to carry a Swiss Made designation, regulated to +/-5 seconds/day with a 42-hour power reserve. The 316L stainless steel case has a scratch-resistant hardening coating and a double-domed sapphire crystal with interior anti-reflective coating, rated to 10 ATM (100m). It ships on a color-matched leather strap, with an optional H-link steel bracelet available as an add-on.

watches.crystal: Domed sapphire, interior AR coating, front and rear
watches.description: The Touring GMT runs on the Miyota 9075 'True GMT' automatic movement, regulated in-house to +/-10 seconds/day at 28,800 vph with a 42-hour power reserve. The 39mm 316L stainless steel case is 46mm lug-to-lug and 12mm thick, with a screw-down crown and screw-down case back rated to 10 ATM (100m), and sapphire crystal with interior anti-reflective coating front and rear. Season Two's four colorways are named for guitars/guitarists (Clapton's Slowhand, Hendrix's Izabella, McCartney's Lefty, Cobain's Daphne) and each ships on a matching leather strap with contrast stitching.

watches.crystal: Sapphire, interior AR coating, front and rear
watches.description: The Daytripper is powered by a Miyota 9075 automatic movement reworked to spotlight its quick-set hour hand, letting wearers jump the local hour forward or back without disturbing the running minutes or seconds, at 28,800 vph with a 42-hour power reserve. The 39mm 316L stainless steel case (12mm thick, 46mm lug-to-lug) has a scratch-resistant hardening coating and sapphire crystal front and rear, rated to 10 ATM (100m). Season Two adds a fully lumed BGW9 Super-LumiNova chapter ring and four gradient dials, each named for a different time of day, on a color-matched leather strap.

The S.O.E. Roadster was Trafford's debut model (originally Kickstarter-funded), inspired by three 1930s Grand Prix drivers -- Robert Benoist, Jean-Pierre Wimille, and William Grover-Williams -- who were recruited into Britain's WWII-era Special Operations Executive, with each colorway named for one of them and finished in his signature racing color. It runs on a Seiko VK64 meca-quartz chronograph movement with a sweeping central chronograph hand and a 24-hour subdial, visually inspired by the Bugatti Type 35 race car. The 43mm case (41mm excluding the bezel) is 48mm lug-to-lug and about 12.8mm thick, with hands and markers lumed in BGW9 Super-LumiNova, rated to 100m water resistance, and ships with both a leather strap and a matching nylon strap.

The Valiant is William Wood's flagship automatic diving watch, built around a 41mm 316L stainless steel case, a Seiko NH35 automatic movement with a 41-hour power reserve, and a double-domed sapphire crystal tinted blue in tribute to founder Jonny Garrett's grandfather's 'Blue Watch' shift pattern at the Newcastle & Gateshead Fire Brigade. Its crown is cast from brass melted down from a genuine 100-year-old British firefighter's helmet, and every colorway ships on a quick-release strap made from upcycled fire hose or a stainless bracelet. The dial's chequered seconds track is lifted from the livery on the side of a British fire engine, and the case is rated to 100m water resistance.

watches.crystal: Sapphire, low-flush curve, anti-reflective coating, blue-tinted
watches.description: The Chivalrous is William Wood's dress-watch line, pairing a 41mm stainless (or rose-gold-plated) case with a Swiss-Made Ronda 762 quartz movement and a sunburst dial set with a Roman numeral at 12. Its signature detail is a commemorative coin let into the caseback, cast from brass melted down from an original 1920s British firefighter's helmet and sealed under its own sapphire window. Finished with a low-flush, anti-reflective sapphire crystal and offered on fire-hose, leather, or steel-bracelet straps, it carries a 50m water resistance rating.

watches.caseMaterial: 316L stainless steel (gold-plated on the Vintage Edition)
watches.crystal: Sapphire, low-flush curve, anti-reflective coating, blue-tinted
watches.description: The Triumph is William Wood's chronograph collection, cased at 41mm in 316L stainless steel (gold-plated on the Vintage Edition) around a Swiss-Made Sellita SW510 chronograph movement with a 48-hour power reserve and brass chronograph pushers. Twin sub-dials at 3 and 9 o'clock are styled after fire-hose pressure-pump gauges, and the seconds track reproduces the chequered pattern from the side of a British fire engine. Rated to 100m water resistance under a double anti-reflective sapphire crystal, it ships on fire-hose, fire-gear, or steel-bracelet straps depending on colorway.

watches.caseMaterial: Stainless steel with black PVD coating
watches.crystal: Sapphire, anti-reflective coating, blue-tinted, exhibition caseback window
watches.description: The Fearless is William Wood's smallest and stealthiest model, a 39mm stainless steel case finished in matte black PVD around a Seiko NH35 automatic movement with a 41-hour power reserve. Its exhibition sapphire caseback is engraved with a modern British firefighter's helmet motif, and the crown is cast from brass melted down from a genuine 100-year-old firefighter's helmet, carrying the same material story used on the Valiant. Charcoal-effect dials with rose-gold, lume-filled indices sit under an anti-reflective, blue-tinted sapphire crystal, and the case is rated to 100m water resistance.

The Metric is Brew Watch Co.'s flagship chronograph, built around a cushion-shaped case that recalls 1970s motorsport chronographs, blending brushed and polished stainless steel 316L for contrast. It runs a Hybrid VK68 meca-quartz chronograph movement behind a sapphire crystal, in a 36mm-wide by 41.5mm lug-to-lug case rated to 5ATM (50m). It anchors Brew's lineup and is offered in the widest range of colorways of any Brew model, from mirror steel and PVD black to gold-toned and patterned dials.

Metric Lite is Brew's most compact watch, reworking the Metric's tonneau case into a clean three-hand, 30mm-wide (34mm lug-to-lug) piece with no chronograph complication. It swaps the flagship's meca-quartz module for an automatic 6T27 movement, keeping the same sapphire crystal, stainless steel 316L case, and 5ATM (50m) water resistance. Colorways lean toward quieter, vintage-inspired dials: brushed steel, PVD gold, and gemstone finishes such as tiger's eye, mother-of-pearl, and Tahitian pearl.

Super Metric shares the standard Metric's 36mm by 41.5mm cushion case, Hybrid VK68 meca-quartz chronograph movement, sapphire crystal, and 5ATM (50m) water resistance, but pushes the dial design further with bold color-blocked chapter rings, dual-finish subdials, and an anodized-aluminum chronograph pusher for a spot of accent color. It reads as the more graphic, motorsport-flavored sibling to the standard Metric dials. Electric Blue, Burnt Orange, Royal Blue, and Lumina Yellow give the line a higher-contrast identity within the Metric family.

The Retrograph is Brew's most literal expression of its coffee-culture identity: Brew built its 38mm by 41.5mm meca-quartz chronograph specifically as a utilitarian, retro timing device whose 60-minute totalizer tracks a 25-35 second espresso shot, alongside other timed durations. The lead Espresso colorway pairs a rosegold PVD-plated stainless steel case with a chocolate-espresso sunburst dial and brown leather strap; sapphire crystal and 5ATM (50m) water resistance carry across the line.

brands.blurb: From the first Japanese wristwatch to the first quartz watch, Seiko has spent more than a century making horology accessible. Its Prospex divers and in-house movements deliver tool-watch credibility at every price.

A Le Locle original and one of the largest Swiss makers, Tissot pairs traditional Swiss manufacturing with aggressive pricing. The integrated-bracelet PRX made 1970s style a modern entry point into mechanical watches.

Born in Lancaster, Pennsylvania and now Swiss-made, Hamilton supplied U.S. military watches through both world wars. The Khaki Field carries that GI heritage, while its movie credits run from 2001 to Interstellar.

Omega timed the first lunar landing and every Olympics for decades. The Speedmaster Professional remains the only watch flight-qualified by NASA for spacewalks, and the co-axial escapement keeps its movements ahead of the pack.
Casio built the indestructible watch. Since 1983 the G-Shock has survived drops, mud, and decades of fashion cycles — the octagonal GA-2100 "CasiOak" turned shock resistance into a style statement.
The jeweler of kings put watches on wrists before most: the Santos (1904) and Tank (1917) defined shaped dress watches. A Cartier is as much design object as timekeeper — which is exactly the point.

Named for the Gold Coast street corner where founder Andrew Perez — an Army veteran and lifelong watch nerd — got his start, Astor + Banks assembles every watch in Chicago. Hardened steel, serious anti-magnetic ratings, and clean officer's-watch design at honest prices.

Studio Underd0g was founded in London in 2020 by industrial designer Richard Benc, who sketched the brand's first watch during the March 2020 lockdown and posted it to a watch-enthusiast Facebook group to an unexpectedly huge response. The name plays on "underdog" — a design-led challenger among the big Swiss houses — with a gamer-style "0" swapped in for the "o". Every watch is hand-assembled start-to-finish by a single technician in Great Britain, and the brand has built its following on pun-named dial colorways (Watermel0n, Pink Lem0nade) and headline collaborations with Moser, Fears, and Massena LAB.

Founded in Paris in 2016 by Etienne Malec, who grew up immersed in his late father's diary cataloguing a lifelong watch collection, Baltic takes its name from his father's roots on Poland's Baltic coast. The brand went public via a Kickstarter campaign in April 2017, pre-selling 1,200 watches of its debut Bicompax and HMS models and raising nearly 8x its goal. Baltic designs vintage-inspired watches assembled and adjusted at its own atelier in Besançon, France, and sells direct-to-consumer to avoid traditional retail markups.

Trafford Watch Co. was founded in 2020 by Nathan Trafford, a designer originally from northern England now based in Austin, Texas, who started the brand to create 'something tangible that lasts.' Its Austin-designed, overseas-manufactured watches lean on narrative color palettes drawn from Texas Hill Country wildflowers (Crossroads), classic rock guitars (Touring GMT), and WWII racing-driver spies (S.O.E. Roadster), earning coverage from Worn & Wound, Watch Clicker, and Teddy Baldassarre.

Founded in 2017 by Jonny Garrett and named for his grandfather William Wood, a Newcastle & Gateshead firefighter who passed away in 2009, the British independent watchmaker builds every piece from genuine upcycled firefighting materials. Straps are cut from retired fire hose, and several collections cast a coin or crown from brass melted down from a century-old firefighter's helmet.

Brew Watch Co. was founded in 2015 by industrial designer Jonathan Ferrer, who launched the brand from New York City via Kickstarter after noticing how baristas timed espresso extraction. Every design decision traces back to that coffee ritual: chronograph totalizers sized to a 25-35 second espresso pour, cushion cases inspired by vintage espresso machines and Art Deco cafes, and a coffee-bean logomark on the crown and dial.
